data.title: U.S. said to delay weapons deliveries to some European countries
data.description:
The U.S. informed some ​European officials that certain previously contracted weapons deliveries are likely to be delayed as the Iran war strains its arms stockpiles, five sources familiar with the matter told Reuters.

Many European countries are expected to be affected, including in the Baltic region and in Scandinavia, some of which share a border with Russia.

Some of the weapons likely to be delayed were bought by European countries under the Foreign Military Sales program, through which foreign nations purchase U.S.-made arms with the U.S. government's ​logistical assistance and consent.

The Trump administration has pushed European allies to buy more U.S.-made weapons and spend more on their own defense instead of relying on the U.S.

But these weapons deliveries are often delayed, pushing some European officials to look at weapons systems manufactured in Europe.

According to U.S. officials, the weapons are needed for the Iran war and they faulted European countries for not helping the U.S. open the Strait of Hormuz.

The U.S. had already drawn down billions of dollars' ⁠worth of weapons stockpiles since Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022 and Israel's military operations in Gaza in late 2023, the report noted.

data.title: Sony to get up to $380M Japanese subsidy for image sensor plant
data.description:
The Japanese government will provide ​subsidies of up to ‌60B yen (about $380M) to Sony (SONY) for an ​image sensor facility ​in the prefecture ⁠of Kumamoto, Reuters reported, citing industry ​minister Ryosei Akazawa.

Image sensors will be "indispensable for autonomous driving and physical ​AI" and "we hope ​a stable supply of image ‌sensors... ⁠will be secured," said Akazawa at a press conference, the report added.

Sony did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Sony is a ​leading maker ​of ⁠image sensors used in smartphones, besides its ​other product portfolio ⁠in entertainment, including games, movies, music, and ⁠anime.

data.title: Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson declares SEK 1.50 semi-annual dividend; launches major SEK 15B share repurchase
data.description:
• Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (ERIC) declares SEK1.50/share semi-annual dividend, in line with previous.
  • Payable Oct. 2; for shareholders of record Sept. 29; ex-div Sept. 29.
  • The Annual General Meeting ((AGM)) also authorized the board of directors to buy back ordinary Class B shares, ensuring the company's holdings do not exceed 10 percent of all shares.
  • On April 16, 2026, the board announced a share buyback program for up to SEK 15 billion on Nasdaq Stockholm, starting no earlier than April 23, 2026, and ending by March 31, 2027.
  • See ERIC Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: NFLX, AA, and more
data.description:
Stock futures inched up early Friday while oil slipped as optimism built that the Middle East conflict could ease following a 10-day Lebanon-Israel cease-fire and President Trump's signal of possible U.S.-Iran weekend talks.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Blaize (BZAI) +22% - Shares surged after announcing a contract worth up to $50M with NeoTensr to co-develop AI edge data center infrastructure in the Asia-Pacific region, with the potential to generate significant revenue within the first year. The deal includes co-branded AI servers and full-stack solutions capable of handling high-volume data workloads, underscoring strong demand for edge AI compute.

Biggest stock losers

  • Netflix (NFLX) -10% - Shares plunged despite a Q1 beat as softer guidance disappointed investors, with the FY2026 revenue outlook maintained at $50.7B–$51.7B (midpoint $51.2B), below consensus, and the operating margin guided at 31.5% vs. 32% expectations, while the Q2 margin is seen declining ~150 bps Y/Y due to higher content amortization. Additionally, co-founder Reed Hastings will not seek re-election as board chairman, adding to the cautious sentiment.
  • Trevi Therapeutics (TRVI) -7% - Shares declined after the company announced a $150M underwritten equity offering, raising dilution concerns, with an additional $22.5M greenshoe option. The company said proceeds will fund development of its lead candidate, Haduvio, for chronic cough conditions.
  • Alcoa (AA) -3% - Shares dipped after Q1 results missed expectations, with EPS of $1.40 and revenue of $3.19B both below consensus, while the outlook pointed to mixed trends with higher tariff costs and energy pressures offsetting some operational improvements. The company maintained its 2026 production guidance but flagged near-term EBITDA headwinds, even as it explores asset monetization opportunities, including advanced talks to sell its Messina East smelter site for a data center project.

data.title: Europe stocks rebound amid Middle East peace signals
data.description:
London (UKX) -0.14% to 10,575. 

Germany (DAX:IND) +0.46% to 24,266. 

France (CAC:IND) +0.34% to 8,290. 

In other parts of Europe, Italy recorded a current account surplus of €3.6B in February.

Slovakia’s current account deficit narrowed to €397.9M in February.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) traded 0.09% higher to 617.5, hovering around the flatline. In broader markets, France and the UK are chairing a meeting of roughly 40 countries. The objective is to demonstrate to the U.S. that key allies are prepared to actively support efforts to restore freedom of navigation in the Strait once security conditions permit.

Investors adopted a cautious stance ahead of the weekend while awaiting clearer signals on US-Iran peace negotiations. UK natural gas futures fell toward 105 pence per therm, hovering near their lowest level in more than six weeks, as hopes grew for an end to the Middle East war. 

In the bond market, the yield on the US 10-year Treasury was almost flat at 4.31%.

UK's 10-year yield was flat at 4.85%. 

Germany's 10-year yield was almost flat at 3.03%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

data.title: Netflix slide puts spotlight on ETFs with the biggest exposure to the stock
data.description:

The streaming giant is down ~10% in Friday's premarket trading and carries a Hold rating from SA Quant.

data.title: Tesla looking to hire chip engineers for Taiwan AI chip complex
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) is seeking semiconductor engineers for its Terafab AI chip complex in Taiwan, according to job ​postings on its website.

The automaker is looking to hire multiple senior process engineers, a process integration engineer, and a yield and metrology engineer.

“We are building Terafab, a vertically integrated semiconductor factory at an unprecedented scale. The facility houses logic, memory, packaging, testing, and lithography mask production under one roof, optimized for rapid iteration and maximum compute density per square foot,” Tesla said in its job descriptions.

Tesla is seeking candidates in roles that will include building processes across three distinct chip families: edge-inference processors, space-hardened chips for orbital satellites, and high-bandwidth memory.

The roles also reference 2nm-class chip dimensions and multiple core front-end fabrication steps, including lithography, etching, thin films, and chemical mechanical planarization.

data.title: Stock index futures muted as Trump signals Iran war may end soon
data.description:
Stock index futures were muted on Friday as President Donald Trump signaled that the U.S. and Iran could hold talks over the weekend, boosting optimism that Middle East tensions may be easing.

Dow futures (INDU) rose 0.27%, while S&P 500 futures (SPX) edged up 0.11% and Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) slipped 0.04%. 

Sentiment improved after a 10-day ceasefire between Lebanon and Israel took effect, with Trump expressing confidence that an agreement could soon be reached to end the Iran conflict and urging the Tehran-aligned Hezbollah group to hold its fire.

U.S. Treasury yields edged lower across the curve. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 1.3 basis points to 4.31%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) slipped 0.4 basis points to 3.78%. The 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) declined 1 basis point to 4.93%.

Investors were preparing for a busy schedule of Federal Reserve commentary, with speeches expected from San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, Richmond Fed President Thomas Barkin, and Fed Governor Christopher Waller.

Top gainers in premarket trading included NiSource (NI) +2.64%, NRG Energy (NRG) +2.08%, and Aon (AON) +1.99%. Decliners included DaVita (DVA) -2.79%, FirstEnergy (FE) -1.85%, and Aflac (AFL) -1.77%.

data.title: U.S. concerned about Russian space nukes, but China is biggest threat - SPACECOM chief
data.description:
Gen. Stephen Whiting, head of U.S. Space Command, said reports of Russia planning a nuclear weapon in space are "so concerning," flagging the risks to global communications and GPS services.

"Russia remains a very historic and sophisticated space power," Whiting told The Times during a podcast appearance. "They've been hurt by economic sanctions, but they continue to invest in counterspace weapons, with the most concerning reports being that they are potentially thinking about placing in orbit a nuclear ASAT [anti-satellite] weapon."

"It would hold at risk everyone's satellites in low earth orbit and that would be an outcome that we just couldn't tolerate," he warned.

When asked if he saw hard evidence of Russia's plan, Whiting said, "I won't speak about our intelligence sources and methods, but it's a report that we're very concerned about."

The official noted how Russia has been targeting satellite systems amid the Ukraine war. "Clearly across Europe, we have seen sustained satellite communication jamming and GPS jamming."

"The problem with that GPS jamming, for example, is it's being done in a way that's affecting civil aviation in Eastern Europe and across southern Europe," he added. "We do not want to see this normalization of trying to interfere with other satellites."

Even so, Whiting said China poses the biggest threat to the U.S. in space, followed by Russia.

"China has moved breathtakingly fast in space," he said. "They have built out this suite of counterspace weapons to hold at risk U.S. and allied space capabilities."

These include cyberattack weapons, satellite communications and GPS jammers, directed energy weapons, and anti-satellite rockets.

China "watched how we used space to make our conventional forces more effective and they have done the exact same thing," Whiting said, adding that the country "developed a space-based targeting system to track and target U.S. and allied conventional forces that are trying to maneuver around the Indo-Pacific area."

data.title: Apple's iPhone shipments in China rise 20% in Q1: Counterpoint
data.description:
Apple’s (AAPL) iPhone shipments rose 20% in China in the first quarter, driven by sustained strong performance of the iPhone 17 series, promotional price cuts, and government subsidies, according to preliminary data from Counterpoint Research’s Market Monitor Tracker.

The company ranked second with a 19% market share, just behind Huawei, which led China’s smartphone market in Q1 2026 with a 20% share—its highest level since Q4 2020. Shipments of the Enjoy 90 series toward the end of Q1 further helped Huawei maintain its leading position, with the brand’s shipments up 2% Y/Y.

“Apple is widely viewed as best positioned to navigate the ongoing global memory crunch, supported by its premium product portfolio and strong supply chain management. In the near-to-medium term, it is more likely to absorb rising costs internally and expand its market share,” Counterpoint said.

 

 

OPPO ranked third in Q1 with a 16% market share, while vivo’s shipments grew 2% Y/Y in Q1, driven by strong performance of low-to-mid-end models such as the Y50, Y500, and S50.

Overall, China’s smartphone shipments fell 4% Y/Y in Q1 2026, mainly due to a high base effect from last year’s government subsidy program, as well as rising costs this year.

The country’s smartphone shipments are expected to decline 9% in 2026, but it will still outperform the global average, Counterpoint added. “This year, smartphone OEMs are facing a “double hit” of shrinking shipments and thinning profit margins, with elevated memory costs likely to persist through 2026. As OEMs raise prices on both new and legacy models, the market demand is expected to soften further, with a modest recovery likely in early June, supported by 618 shopping promotions.”
